nodes.title: MVP must include the full Habit lifecycle, not just init
nodes.rationale: Recovery and self-evolution are what make a Habit different from a static skill. They have to be wired in from day one even if simple, otherwise the MVP doesn't test the real shape.
alternatives: ship a design-only initializer first and add runtime/recovery/evolve later
split MVP into two milestones: lifecycle bones now, evolution later

nodes.title: Six-command lifecycle: init / materialize / next / record / recover / evolve
nodes.rationale: Smallest complete set of verbs that proves the loop end to end for a Habit built from scratch.
alternatives: collapse next + record into a single 'step' command
drop materialize and run the contract directly
fold recover into next as a special-case response

nodes.title: Evolution is manual and reviewable, not autonomous
nodes.rationale: Autonomous self-modification is too risky for v1 and would couple the MVP to trust/safety problems that aren't the point of the experiment.
alternatives: auto-apply low-risk proposals without review
skip evolve entirely for MVP and add it after first real Habit lands

nodes.title: Two layers: common substrate (system-owned) and habit-specific contract (user-owned)
nodes.rationale: Clear ownership boundary lets the system harden machinery once while each Habit defines its own routine, gates, recovery, and evolution.
alternatives: single-layer Habit definition without a substrate
three-layer split with a separate runtime/router tier

nodes.title: What concrete file formats does the habit-specific contract accept?
nodes.prompt: The spec says the contract can mix Markdown, YAML, Glyph, Python adapters, or other code. For the MVP, which combination does the first materializer actually have to consume?

options.label: Markdown + YAML only
options.body: MVP materializer reads guided Markdown contract files plus a small set of YAML configs. No code execution. Glyph and Python adapters are deferred.

options.label: Markdown + YAML + optional Python helper module
options.body: MVP materializer reads Markdown + YAML and optionally loads a single Python module that exposes deterministic helpers. No Glyph yet.

options.label: Full mix from day one
options.body: MVP materializer must handle Markdown, YAML, Glyph, and Python adapters together. Higher cost; broader coverage of real Habits.

nodes.title: MVP scope: what's in, what's out, no skill-import converter yet
nodes.rationale: Locks the surface area of v1. In scope is the bare-minimum loop plus a simple test harness and one canonical example. Out of scope explicitly excludes autonomous self-modification, state-machine compilation, cross-project trace mining, DSPy/model-judgment nodes, marketplace packaging, and general arbitrary scripting. The MVP also intentionally does not ship an `import-skill` converter — existing skills are reference material, not auto-converted Habits.
alternatives: include an import-skill converter in v1 to seed the first Habits from existing tooling
drop the simple test harness from v1 scope and rely on manual ad-hoc testing
include a richer state-machine language to broaden what the materializer can compile

nodes.title: Does `habit init` need an interactive interview or are guided files enough?
nodes.prompt: The spec leans toward 'files are enough if they are clear and complete.' Confirm: is the MVP allowed to ship without any interactive prompts, or do certain contract sections need a guided Q&A?

options.label: Files only
options.body: MVP generates the contract folder with guided question prose inside each file. The user fills them in offline. No interactive prompts.

options.label: Interactive for core sections
options.body: MVP prompts interactively for routine.md, gates.md, and recovery.md (the highest-stakes contract pieces), and leaves the rest as files.

nodes.title: What counts as a 'constrained contract format' for the first materializer?
nodes.prompt: The materializer doesn't need to support arbitrary logic or Glyph compilation. Define the constraint: which contract patterns is it required to handle, and which does it explicitly reject with a clear error?

options.label: Linear phases + required-evidence gates only
options.body: Materializer compiles an ordered list of phases, each with required-evidence gates and a single instruction packet. No branching, no parallel phases.

options.label: Linear phases with simple conditional branches
options.body: Like the linear option, but phases may branch on a single ledger-field condition. Still no parallel phases and no loops.

nodes.title: First recovery system may not cover enough failure modes to feel useful
nodes.mitigation: Enumerate the seven p2p recovery codes (unsupported terminal, own surface unresolved, name collision, peer not found, stale peer surface, bootstrap parse failure, send-buffer failure) and require structured handoffs for each before declaring MVP done. Track real failures in the ledger and let `habit evolve` surface gaps.

nodes.title: Does `habit evolve` apply approved patches or just write proposal files?
nodes.prompt: The spec says applying the change 'can be manual' for the MVP. Confirm whether the MVP writes a reviewable proposal and stops, or whether it can apply the patch after explicit user approval.

options.label: Proposal only
options.body: `habit evolve` writes the proposal file and stops. The user applies the patch by hand. Lowest blast radius.

options.label: Approve-then-apply
options.body: `habit evolve` writes the proposal. A separate command (or flag) applies it after explicit user approval. Tighter loop, slightly more machinery.

nodes.title: p2p is the canonical MVP Habit
nodes.rationale: p2p already strains as a skill: stateful identity, fragile transport, repeatable failures with structured recovery, natural substrate/contract split. It's the highest-signal first Habit.
alternatives: tfork (terminal forking) as the canonical first Habit
a synthetic toy Habit with no real-world consumer

nodes.title: p2p MVP slice is registered-peer messaging only
nodes.rationale: Connect-to-existing and spawn-new add fork-terminal and bootstrap-timing dependencies that would dominate the MVP. Registered-peer messaging exercises identity, routing, recovery, and evolution without that overhead.
alternatives: include connect-to-existing-peer in the first slice
include spawn-new-peer (and therefore fork-terminal) in the first slice
ship the full p2p surface at once

nodes.title: Habituation may not actually produce useful evolution proposals
nodes.mitigation: Use the p2p Habit on real cases for at least two weeks before judging. If `habit evolve p2p` cannot generate reviewable, specific proposals from accumulated ledger events, treat that as a failure of the contract design rather than of the user — iterate on contract shape and proposal heuristics.

nodes.title: Definition of done: eight concrete success criteria
nodes.rationale: Each criterion maps to one lifecycle verb plus the canonical example. Together they assert that the full loop is executable end-to-end against p2p, not just that the schemas exist on disk.
alternatives: judge MVP success qualitatively after a real two-week dogfood instead of against a fixed checklist
drop the test-harness criterion and judge the loop by demo only
require a second canonical Habit beyond p2p before declaring done

nodes.title: Build order: schemas, init, materialize, next/record, recover, evolve, p2p example
nodes.rationale: Substrate schemas anchor every later piece. Init and materialize are pure file-generation and the cheapest to land. next/record produces the first runnable loop. recover and evolve plug into the ledger that next/record builds. p2p is the integration test for everything.
alternatives: build p2p end-to-end first, then generalize the substrate
build evolve before recover (evolve over ledger doesn't strictly require recover to exist)
